prompt:
Add two knowledge nodes to the-valley's graph capturing project-model decisions the owner (patflynn) ratified on 2026-07-06. Read .the-valley/README.md first for the node convention (one file per node, YAML frontmatter, ids derived from slugs — inspect how existing dcr-*/ida-* files derive their 7-hex ids and match that exactly). Context: the repo is gaining a CUE schema where the unit of declaration is a 'project' (a concurrent PR by another agent adds it; you do not depend on it — do not touch flake/schema files).

Node 1 — decision (status: decided, created 2026-07-06) in .the-valley/decisions/: a valley project is 1:1 with a git repo. The project's single store is one bare repo named <project>.git; project name and repo name coincide. Rationale: fundamentally and ergonomically simpler — one name, one clone, one knowledge graph, one migration unit — chosen consciously for simplicity's sake, not as a claim that it is the only way people model projects. Consequence: multi-repo or repo-less projects are out of scope until a rung demands them.

Node 2 — idea (status: adopted, created 2026-07-06) in .the-valley/ideas/: a project's identity transcends the host that serves it. Today the host's valley.cue declares which projects it serves, but the direction is self-describing projects — a project's own declaration travels in its store, the way the .the-valley/ knowledge graph already does, and host config shrinks to a serving list. Open design questions to name in the body (not resolve): where the in-store declaration lives and its schema; how a host serving-list references a project; how project names resolve across hosts — link that last one to the existing federation idea node in .the-valley/ideas/ (the ida-* node about federation groups) with a [[id]] wiki-link, and link the two new nodes to each other.

Nothing else changes — no design/ docs, no other nodes, no code. Match existing node style and brevity; verify relative links resolve. Open a PR (do not merge); PR description: one line per node stating what it records.
